title: Anesthesia for Endocrinal Disorders MCQ – Chapter 23
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: What are the basic goals of preoperative evaluation in a diabetic patient?

options.B: Assess severity by blood sugar & HbA1c, rule out autonomic and peripheral neuropathy, and organ damage

questions.explanation: The basic goals are to assess severity by checking blood sugar and HbA1c, rule out autonomic neuropathy, peripheral neuropathy, and organ damage especially nephropathy.

questions.question: Why should premedication against aspiration be given to diabetic patients?

options.A: They have increased gastric acid production

questions.explanation: Diabetic patients have delayed gastric emptying, therefore premedication against aspiration should be given.

questions.question: How long before surgery should sulfonyl urea and metformin be stopped?
questions.explanation: Due to long half-lives, sulfonyl urea and metformin should be stopped 24–48 hours prior to surgery.

questions.question: In the traditional insulin regime, what fraction of the AM dose of insulin is given on the morning of surgery?
questions.explanation: The most widespread traditionally employed regime is to give 1/2 of the AM dose of insulin on the morning of surgery after starting 5% dextrose infusion.

questions.question: At what rate should 5% dextrose be infused when giving insulin on the morning of surgery?
questions.explanation: 5% dextrose is infused at a rate of 125 mL/hour to avoid the possibility of hypoglycemia.

questions.question: By how much does 1 unit of regular insulin decrease blood sugar?
questions.explanation: 1 unit of insulin decreases blood sugar levels by 25–30 mg/dL.

questions.question: What is the formula for regular insulin infusion rate (units/hour) as per current guidelines?
questions.explanation: Insulin is given by the formula: Units/hour = plasma glucose (mg/dL)/150.

questions.question: What is the target plasma sugar level during the perioperative period?
questions.explanation: The target should be to keep plasma sugar level between 150 and 180 mg/dL.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of death in the perioperative period in diabetic patients?
questions.explanation: Myocardial ischemia is the most common cause of death in the perioperative period in diabetic patients.

questions.question: Why may MI go silent in the postoperative period in diabetic patients?
questions.explanation: The MI may go silent in postoperative period if the patient has autonomic neuropathy.

questions.question: Which patients with diabetes can safely receive regional anesthesia?

options.B: Patients not having peripheral and/or autonomic neuropathy

questions.explanation: Patients not having peripheral and/or autonomic neuropathy can safely receive regional anesthesia.

questions.question: Why may difficult intubation be anticipated in diabetic patients?

options.B: Due to involvement of atlanto-occipital and temporomandibular joint (stiff joint syndrome)

questions.explanation: Difficult intubation may be anticipated due to involvement of atlanto-occipital and temporomandibular joint (stiff joint syndrome).

questions.question: What may act as a savior when hypotension and bradycardia in diabetic patients with autonomic neuropathy do not respond to atropine and ephedrine?
questions.explanation: Hypotension and bradycardia in patients with autonomic neuropathy may not respond to atropine and ephedrine; intravenous adrenaline may act as savior.

questions.question: Which of the following drugs should be avoided in diabetic patients because they cause sympathetic stimulation and hyperglycemia?
questions.explanation: Drugs causing sympathetic stimulation like ketamine, pancuronium, atropine, desflurane >6% should be avoided as they cause hyperglycemia.

questions.question: When should elective surgery be performed in a hyperthyroid patient?
questions.explanation: Elective surgery should be deferred till the patient is euthyroid.

questions.question: What should be brought down to normal with beta blockers before taking a hyperthyroid patient for surgery?
questions.explanation: Sleeping pulse rate should be brought down to normal with beta blockers before taking the patient for surgery.

questions.question: Why should airway assessment be done in hyperthyroid patients with large thyroid?

options.B: Large thyroid can produce tracheal compression

questions.explanation: As large thyroid can produce tracheal compression, airway assessment by indirect laryngoscopy, X-ray neck or CT scan is must.

questions.question: Why should local anesthetics with adrenaline NOT be used in hyperthyroid patients?
questions.explanation: Regional anesthesia can be safely given in hyperthyroid patients however local anesthetics with adrenaline must not be used as they cause sympathetic stimulation.

questions.question: Why are central neuraxial blocks preferred in hyperthyroid patients?
questions.explanation: Central neuraxial blocks are preferred because of their capacity to block the sympathetic response.

questions.question: Which is the induction agent of choice in hyperthyroid patients and why?
questions.explanation: As thiopentone possesses antithyroid property, it is the induction agent of choice for hyperthyroid patients.

questions.question: What is the preferred maintenance agent for hyperthyroid patients prone to arrhythmias?
questions.explanation: As hyperthyroid patients are more prone for arrhythmias, most cardiac stable i.e. Isoflurane should be the preferred agent.

questions.question: Does the MAC of inhalational agents change in hyperthyroidism?

options.D: It varies depending on the agent

questions.explanation: Minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) of inhalational agents remains same in hyperthyroidism.

questions.question: What is a thyroid storm and when does it most often occur?

options.B: A life-threatening condition most often occurring in postoperative period

questions.explanation: Thyroid storm is a life-threatening condition most often occurring in postoperative period, however can occur intraoperatively also.

questions.question: Which drug is used in thyroid storm to block conversion of T4 to T3?
questions.explanation: Management of thyroid storm includes dexamethasone which blocks conversion of T4 to T3.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of upper airway obstruction after thyroid surgery?
questions.explanation: Upper airway obstruction is most often due to tracheal compression caused by expanding hematoma, treated immediately by opening the sutures.

questions.question: What can hypoparathyroidism after thyroidectomy cause?
questions.explanation: Hypocalcemia caused by hypoparathyroidism can precipitate laryngospasm.

questions.question: What is the emergency treatment of choice for a hyperthyroid patient undergoing emergency surgery?

options.B: IV esmolol or propranolol to settle tachycardia

questions.explanation: At least tachycardia should be settled with IV esmolol or IV propranolol. Propranolol has the added advantage of inhibiting conversion of T4 to T3.

questions.question: Should elective surgery be deferred in all hypothyroid patients?

options.B: No, mild hypothyroidism should not be a deterrent to postpone the surgery

questions.explanation: Ideally elective surgery should be deferred till euthyroid, however mild hypothyroidism should not be a deterrent to postpone the surgery.

questions.question: Which of the following are associated conditions with hypothyroidism that should be ruled out preoperatively?

options.B: Congestive cardiac failure, anemia, hypothermia, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia

questions.explanation: Associated conditions like CCF, anemia, hypothermia, hyponatremia, and hypoglycemia should be ruled out.

questions.question: Why should premedication with benzodiazepines be avoided in hypothyroid patients?

options.C: These patients are extremely sensitive to depressant drugs

questions.explanation: Hypothyroid patients are extremely sensitive to depressant drugs therefore premedication with benzodiazepines should be avoided.

questions.question: What is the induction agent of choice in hypothyroid patients with normal cardiac output?
questions.explanation: Induction is normally done with propofol; however, if there is evidence of low cardiac output then ketamine is preferred.

questions.question: Which muscle relaxants are preferred in hypothyroid patients?
questions.explanation: As mivacurium, atracurium/Cis-atracurium do not have the possibility of accumulation, they are preferred muscle relaxants in hypothyroidism.

questions.question: Why should all drugs be used in minimal possible doses in hypothyroid patients?

options.C: The metabolism of drugs is reduced in hypothyroidism

questions.explanation: As the metabolism of drugs is reduced in hypothyroidism, they should be used in minimal possible doses.

questions.question: What additional cover is needed for emergency surgery in severely hypothyroid patients?

options.B: IV T3 and T4, and steroid cover

questions.explanation: IV T3 and T4 should be given, and as adrenal function is often decreased, steroid cover also becomes a must.

questions.question: What drug is used to stabilize hypertension preoperatively in pheochromocytoma?
questions.explanation: Patient's hypertension must be stabilized by alpha blocker; most often used is phenoxybenzamine.

questions.question: Why must beta blockers NOT be started before alpha blockers in pheochromocytoma?

options.B: Unopposed alpha action can cause malignant hypertension
options.D: Alpha blockers take longer to act

questions.explanation: Beta blockers must not be started before initiating therapy with alpha blocker otherwise unopposed action can cause malignant hypertension.

questions.question: Why is halothane absolutely contraindicated in pheochromocytoma?
questions.explanation: Halothane is absolutely contraindicated because it sensitizes myocardium to catecholamines.

questions.question: What can happen during tumor handling in pheochromocytoma surgery?

options.B: Profound hypertension and life-threatening arrhythmias due to increased catecholamines

questions.explanation: Increase in catecholamines during tumor handling can cause profound hypertension and life-threatening arrhythmias.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of death in the immediate postoperative period in pheochromocytoma patients?
questions.explanation: Hypotension is the most common cause of death in immediate postoperative period in pheochromocytoma patients, caused by abrupt withdrawal of catecholamines after ligation of tumor vein.

questions.question: What should be started immediately after tumor vein ligation in pheochromocytoma surgery?
questions.explanation: Dextrose normal saline should be started immediately after vein ligation to prevent hypotension and hypoglycemia.

questions.question: For how long should hydrocortisone be continued after unilateral adrenalectomy for pheochromocytoma?
questions.explanation: Hydrocortisone (100 mg/day) is continued for 1–2 days for unilateral and 3–6 days for bilateral adrenalectomy.

questions.question: What effects of cortisol must be corrected before elective surgery in Cushing syndrome?

options.B: Hypertension, hyperglycemia, volume overload, and hypokalemia

questions.explanation: Hypertension, hyperglycemia, volume overload and hypokalemia must be corrected before elective surgery.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of secondary adrenal insufficiency?
questions.explanation: Exogenous steroids are the most common cause of secondary adrenal insufficiency.

questions.question: Any patient who has taken steroids (prednisone >5 mg/day) for more than how many weeks in the last 1 year must receive intraoperative steroid supplementation?
questions.explanation: Any patient who has taken steroid for more than 3 weeks in the last 1 year must receive intraoperative supplementation with hydrocortisone.

questions.question: What is the dose of hydrocortisone supplementation for patients with adrenal insufficiency?
questions.explanation: 100 mg of hydrocortisone 8 hourly beginning on the day of surgery and continued for 24–48 hours; continuous infusion at 10 mg/hour is considered better.

questions.question: Why is etomidate contraindicated in adrenal insufficiency?
questions.explanation: Etomidate is contraindicated as it causes adrenocortical suppression.

questions.question: Why should sevoflurane be avoided in mineralocorticoid excess with hypokalemic nephropathy?

options.B: It produces fluoride that can worsen nephropathy

questions.explanation: Sevoflurane should be avoided if there is hypokalemic nephropathy due to its fluoride production.

questions.question: What is the most important concern for acromegaly patients undergoing anesthesia?
questions.explanation: The most important concern for acromegaly patients is difficult airway management due to prognathism, macroglossia, overgrowth of epiglottis, and narrowed glottis.

questions.question: How long does recovery of hypothalamic-pituitary axis (HPA) take after exogenous steroid use?
questions.explanation: Recovery of HPA takes 1 year after exogenous steroid use.

questions.question: What can happen in hypothyroid patients in the postoperative period due to hypoventilation?
questions.explanation: Hypothyroid patients can go into hypoxia in postoperative period due to hypoventilation.
